title: Percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy and thyroid isthmus penetration (PETRIS)
authors: Rára A, Soták M, Hynek M, Kovačič K, Filipovský T, Tyll T
one_line_summary: In this single-center prospective study of 100 percutaneous dilatational tracheostomies, trans-isthmic puncture occurred in 68% of cases and was not associated with increased early complications, though findings are exploratory and underpowered.
key_pearls: Thyroid isthmus penetration during percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy (PDTS) is common, occurring in **68%** of procedures in this preselected cohort.

Trans-isthmic PDTS was not associated with a higher observed rate of early complications compared to non-trans-isthmic procedures (**4.4% vs 3.1%**; p=1.00), but the study is underpowered to detect small differences.
Pre-procedural ultrasound identified high-risk anatomy and redirected **18%** of initially PDTS-eligible patients to surgical tracheostomy, highlighting its value in patient selection.
Intra-procedural ultrasound guidance did not **significantly** reduce early complications compared to landmark technique (**2.0% vs 6.1%**; p=0.342), though statistical power was limited.
Bronchoscopic inspection prompted guidewire repositioning in **5%** of cases, primarily for off-center positioning or incorrect tracheal ring level.
The observed complication rate was lower for PDTS than surgical tracheostomy (**4.0% vs 23.9%**; p<0.001), but this comparison is confounded by baseline differences in age, BMI, and anatomy.
Prioritizing optimal cannula positioning within the trachea may be more important than avoiding the thyroid isthmus, as attempts to bypass it could result in suboptimal placement.
population: Adult ICU patients requiring tracheostomy for prolonged mechanical ventilation or neurological impairment, preselected by pre-procedural ultrasound to exclude high-risk anatomy

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
- PDTS is a common bedside ICU procedure, considered relatively safe compared to surgical tracheostomy.
- Standard technique relies on anatomical landmarks (larynx, cricoid cartilage, jugulum) with bronchoscopic verification.
- Ultrasound can be used for pre-procedural anatomical assessment and intra-procedural guidance; the TRACHUS trial showed comparable safety and efficacy.
- The **recommended** puncture level (between **second and fourth tracheal rings**) corresponds to the usual location of the thyroid isthmus.
- There is no clear consensus on whether to avoid, disregard, or surgically manage the isthmus during PDTS.
- The clinical relevance of thyroid isthmus penetration and the necessity of avoiding it remain insufficiently defined.
- The study hypothesized that trans-isthmic cannula placement is common and may not increase early complications, and that prioritizing optimal tracheal positioning may be more important than isthmus avoidance.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- **Design**: Single-center, prospective interventional study at Military University Hospital Prague, April 2023–April 2025.
- **Registration**: ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT 05792098).
- **Population**: Adult ICU patients indicated for tracheostomy due to prolonged mechanical ventilation or neurological impairment.
- **Exclusion from PDTS**: Inability to discontinue therapeutic anticoagulation/antiplatelet therapy; extensive vascular structures or high-risk anatomy on pre-procedural ultrasound.
- **Pre-procedural ultrasound**: Performed by principal investigator to assess vascular anatomy; **27 of 136 (18%)** initially PDTS-eligible patients were redirected to surgical tracheostomy after ultrasound assessment.
- **Allocation**: Computer-generated sequence (Graphpad.com) assigned eligible patients to Group A (landmark-based, n=51) or Group B (ultrasound-guided, n=49).
- **Technique**: Single-stage dilation (BLUperc kit, Smith Medical); strict percutaneous tracheal puncture.
- **Group B**: Real-time ultrasound used to guide needle insertion for optimal midline and vertical positioning; not used to intentionally avoid the isthmus.
- **Post-puncture ultrasound**: Performed in both groups to document guidewire position and assess thyroid isthmus penetration (transverse and parasagittal planes).
- **Acceptable guidewire position**: Central alignment between **11 and 1 o'clock** on anterior tracheal wall; vertical placement from intercartilaginous space between **1st–2nd** to **4th–5th** tracheal rings.
- **Bronchoscopy**: Ambu aScope 4 Broncho used in all cases for final verification; guidewire repositioned if malpositioned.
- **Post-procedure**: Chest X-ray to confirm cannula placement.
- **Outcomes**: Primary – frequency of thyroid isthmus penetration and association with early complications (bleeding requiring intervention, local infection). Secondary – role of ultrasound in guidewire placement accuracy and patient selection.
- **Statistics**: Mann–Whitney U test for continuous variables, Fisher's exact test for categorical variables; odds ratios with 95% CI; two-tailed p<0.05 **significant**; R software.
section_pearls: Pre-procedural ultrasound screening redirected **18%** of initially eligible patients from PDTS to surgical tracheostomy, demonstrating its role in risk stratification.
Ultrasound guidance in Group B was used to optimize midline and vertical positioning, not to intentionally avoid the thyroid isthmus.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content:
- **Total tracheostomies**: 180 over 2 years; 44 initially surgical, 136 initially PDTS.
- **Final PDTS cohort**: 100 analyzed (51 Group A, 49 Group B); 9 excluded (8 research team absent, 1 missing follow-up); 27 redirected to surgical after ultrasound.
- **Baseline characteristics**: No **significant** differences between Groups A and B in age, BMI, sex, indication, tracheal diameter, or isthmus width. Skin-to-trachea distance was **significantly** shorter in Group B (**10.3 ± 2.9 mm vs 11.6 ± 3.1 mm**; p=0.027, Cohen's d ≈ 0.4).
- **Thyroid isthmus penetration**: Overall **68/100 (68%)** procedures traversed the isthmus (34 in Group A, 34 in Group B).
- **Complications by isthmus status**: Trans-isthmic: 3 complications (1 infection, 2 bleeding) – **4.4%**. Non-trans-isthmic: 1 bleeding – **3.1%**. No **significant** difference (p=1.00, OR=1.43 [95% CI 0.11–77.47]).
- **Complications by group**: Group A: 1 infection (**2.0%**). Group B: 3 mild bleeding events (**6.1%**). No **significant** difference (p=0.342, OR=0.31 [95% CI 0.01–4.02]).
- **Cricoid cartilage fracture**: Observed twice in Group A; classified as intraoperative technical events, not postoperative complications.
- **Bronchoscopic corrections**: Guidewire repositioned in **5 patients** (3 off-center, 2 incorrect ring level). No posterior tracheal wall injury or cranial displacement observed.
- **PDTS vs Surgical**: PDTS complication rate **4.0%** (4/100) vs surgical **23.9%** (17/71); p<0.001, OR=0.13 [95% CI 0.03–0.44]. Descriptive only due to baseline confounding (surgical patients older, higher BMI).
section_pearls: Trans-isthmic puncture occurred in **68%** of PDTS cases, confirming it is a common anatomical occurrence.
The study was underpowered for comparisons between trans-isthmic vs non-trans-isthmic (power <5%) and ultrasound-guided vs landmark techniques (power ~20%).

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- **Primary finding**: Trans-isthmic puncture is common (**68%**) and was not associated with higher observed early complications in this preselected cohort; however, limited power and wide confidence intervals necessitate cautious, exploratory interpretation.
- **Role of pre-procedural ultrasound**: Systematic pre-procedural ultrasound identified high-risk anatomy and redirected **18%** of patients to surgical tracheostomy, reducing baseline procedural risk in the PDTS cohort. This supports ultrasound's primary value in patient selection rather than solely real-time guidance.
- **Intra-procedural ultrasound**: Used to optimize needle insertion for midline alignment and vertical positioning, not to intentionally avoid the isthmus. Prioritizing optimal cannula positioning may be more important than isthmus avoidance, as attempts to bypass it could lead to suboptimal tracheal placement.
- **Clinical implications**: In patients with normal isthmus anatomy, routine avoidance may not be necessary; however, enlarged or vascular isthmi may warrant modified approach or surgical tracheostomy. Findings require confirmation in larger studies.
- **Bronchoscopy role**: Final bronchoscopic inspection led to guidewire repositioning in **5%** of cases, supporting its complementary role when ultrasound visualization is limited or inconclusive. A combined or selective approach may balance safety and resource utilization.
- **Operator considerations**: Ultrasound-guided PDTS is optimally performed by experienced operators; when imaging is clear and guidewire position satisfactory, bronchoscopy may be unnecessary.
- **PDTS vs Surgical comparison**: The lower observed complication rate in PDTS (**4.0% vs 23.9%**) is confounded by baseline differences (age, BMI, anatomy) and should be considered descriptive and hypothesis-generating only.
section_pearls: The primary value of ultrasound in PDTS may lie in pre-procedural anatomical assessment and patient selection, not solely in real-time intra-procedural guidance.
Findings are exploratory and hypothesis-generating; non-significant results should not be interpreted as evidence of equivalence between techniques.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content:
- **Small sample size**: Study adequately powered (>95%) to detect the observed difference between PDTS and surgical tracheostomy (Cohen's h=0.62), but power was ~20% for ultrasound-guided vs landmark comparison (Cohen's h=0.22) and <5% for trans-isthmic vs non-trans-isthmic comparison (Cohen's h=0.06). Wide confidence intervals indicate substantial statistical uncertainty.
- **Early complications only**: Follow-up limited to **10 days**, capturing only immediate postoperative complications. Long-term outcomes (tracheal stenosis, tracheomalacia) were not assessed; it remains uncertain whether trans-isthmic placement affects long-term outcomes.
- **Operator variability**: Procedures performed by physicians with broad range of experience levels, including trainees. No formal interobserver/intraobserver variability assessment for ultrasound interpretation, though consensus review by radiologist and two intensivists was used.
- **Surrogate assessment**: Thyroid isthmus penetration determined by guidewire trajectory as surrogate for final cannula position; minor deviations during dilatation cannot be completely excluded.
- **Single-center design**: Limits generalizability to other institutions with different protocols and operator experience. However, operator variability reflects real-world clinical practice.
- **Confounding in surgical comparison**: Patients undergoing surgical tracheostomy were older, had higher BMI, and more anatomically challenging conditions, introducing substantial confounding.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content:
- Trans-isthmic puncture during PDTS is common (**68%**) and, in this preselected cohort, was not associated with higher observed early complications.
- Findings are exploratory and require confirmation in larger, adequately powered multicenter studies.
- In patients with normal thyroid isthmus anatomy, trans-isthmic tracheostomy may be feasible without apparent increase in early complications.
- Pre-procedural ultrasound is valuable for anatomical assessment and patient selection, particularly for identifying high-risk features warranting alternative approach.
- The additional benefit of intra-procedural ultrasound guidance in reducing early complications was not demonstrated in this study.
- A tailored approach incorporating pre-procedural ultrasound assessment with selective use of intra-procedural imaging and bronchoscopy may represent a rational strategy for optimizing PDTS safety.

strengths_limitations:
**Strengths**: Prospective interventional design with systematic pre-procedural ultrasound assessment in all patients; clear distinction between pre-procedural risk stratification and intra-procedural guidance roles of ultrasound; real-world operator variability reflecting routine clinical practice; bronchoscopic verification in all cases as reference standard; registered clinical trial with predefined outcomes.
**Limitations**: Small sample size with inadequate power for secondary comparisons (ultrasound-guided vs landmark, trans-isthmic vs non-trans-isthmic); single-center design limiting generalizability; short 10-day follow-up excluding long-term complications (tracheal stenosis, tracheomalacia); surrogate assessment of isthmus penetration via guidewire trajectory; unblinded allocation due to nature of intervention; operator experience heterogeneity without formal variability assessment; confounded comparison between PDTS and surgical tracheostomy due to baseline population differences.
